speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess Amy [? had ?] sound to it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it things from all over the world?

speaker: interviewer
text: Speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's April 9th, 1995. This interview-- this interview is with survivor Jack Pomerantz. The interviewer is Steve North. We're in Short Hills, New Jersey, in the US. We'll be conducting this interview in English. Hello, my name is Steve North. I will be interviewing Jack Pomerantz today. We're in his home, which is in Short Hills, New Jersey, in the United States. And today's date is April 9th, 1995. Let's begin with the basic information. Your name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack Pomerantz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell your last name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-O-M-E-R-A-N-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name you were given when you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was P-O-M-E-R-A-N-E-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your first name in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish it's Yankel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yankel. And we would spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Y-A-K-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Or Y-A-N-K-E-L? Yankel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yankel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish was-- was Yankel. It was with the N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Yiddish, Yakel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 5th, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that means that now you are?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be-- May the 5th, I'll be 77 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Young.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you been born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Born in Radzyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we would spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-A-Z-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I've seen the spelling R-A-D-Z-Y-N. OK. And that's, of course, in-- in Poland. In Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Tell me, first, what do you know about that city? What-- what was the Jewish population of the city when you were born?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish population I only-- I don't remember everything exactly-- I think it was, like, 90-- 900 families. Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: 900 Families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And out of the whole town of Radzyn, how many people were Jewish in terms of percent? I'm talking Jewish was 900 families.

speaker: interviewee
text: So out of the whole population of the town, was half the town Jewish?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I would say the-- the town by itself must have been about 70%, 75%, Jewish-- OK. What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and about 25% Christian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Tell me about your family. Tell me about your parents and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My family. My father's name was Abisch. My mother's name was Fryda. I was the youngest one among eight children, which we were-- five brothers and three sisters. And I was the [NON-ENGLISH], because in Jewish you say the [NON-ENGLISH]. We had a-- we had a terrible time, because we were the poorest family in the town. We were so poor that when I went to bed, I couldn't-- not to bed, I slept on the floor. But when I went to sleep, I couldn't fall asleep, because I needed a piece of bread to fill up my stomach. And that happened quite often.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you so poor? What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a peddler. He would go to the farmers in the-- in the outskirts of the town. He would buy different things from them, if it's chickens or eggs or-- or wheat or anything that we were selling. Many times he made a couple dollar, a couple zloty, and many times he didn't. And looks like he couldn't afford that. He just-- he was very religious. His mind was more towards religion than to feed the children. And it was times, as I remember, when it came to a holiday where the neighbors were cookin' for Passover, or Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur-- especially Passover. And I remember distinctly that my mother and my sister would put [? rocks ?] in the pots that the smoke, the steam to go out the windows-- through a window, that the people should think that we have also food for Passover, which we didn't have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't have it. Did you get help from the community or the neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fact is, I just looked in a book. And I saw a man-- I forgot his name now. And he would help us out. But there wasn't too many rich people that they could help. So as much if we could get help, we got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the interesting story is that we were five brothers and three sisters. Well, when we grew up, you would never knew that we come from a family that poor, because every one of us grew up to be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how to explain? One of the nicest people in the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have to tell, also, at times I used to work for a Polish man in the farm. The Polish man was an alcoholic. And he took me many time to his place where he keeps the wheat because his wife had the key. He couldn't get in, because she knew he was alcoholic. So he went in by takin' off some pieces of wood, he could slide in. And he would give it to me to sell it. And then when I sold it, I gave him the money, he bought liquor.

speaker: interviewer
text: But many times, as I say, I couldn't go to bed to sleep-- to sleeps-- because I was empty stomach. So I myself went at night time and stole-- and I have to say, I-- I don't know how to say it? Is it stealing or surviving? And I took some of that wheat, and I sold it. And my happiest day of the month was when I got some money, and I could go to a baker and get a loaf of bread. That's the whole thing I took is to get a loaf of bread, and maybe an additional loaf of bread for the family. Now, how old were you around that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been that time around 16, 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let me go back before that. And tell me what-- what kind of school did you go to as a child? Very bad. I went to Cheder. As we were poor, and there was no food, everything went wrong. Even the school went wrong. I don't remember even going to any public school. The only thing I remember-- There were-- there were public schools, though, in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was. But there also was discrimination about Jewish, you know, against Jews, especially to go high school or something. But anyways, I don't remember exactly. But what I do remember that I didn't have any school, any education. The only education that I have, which I'm reading the English paper, and-- and also I read the German paper-- in Yiddish I read very good-- is from myself. Self-learning.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Cheder you learned how to read and write Yiddish and Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little, yet. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the basics.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the basic. But mostly when I got a little older and I got a [INAUDIBLE], I started to learn to teach myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your house like? Where-- where did this big family live?

speaker: interviewee
text: At one time we lived in one place where my father couldn't pay the rent, so they got him out. [INAUDIBLE] we moved in a Polish family at a little shack in the backyard, which was two little windows. The whole place must have been, I would say, 12 by 16. That was the whole size of the house. And there was two rooms-- a little tiny kitchen and the main-- the bedroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all 10 of you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --lived in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because when we went to sleep on straw on the floor, there was, like, three brothers in one side, and the other two brothers foot-to-foot. And many times my brother would scream, why don't you take your foot out of the my [LAUGHS] mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got to laugh about it. But I can laugh. I'm a proud. You know why? Because what I made out of myself without education. I think I'm educated, but in a different way. Mm-hm. Tell me about your-- your family's religious life.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very, very religious. One time I remember I went with my Polish friends, and they were treating me in a Christian deli for a little deli. Somebody must have saw it and told my father. That's all I needed. I was suffering for months. He kept beating me. I didn't feel my back anymore. Why I ate that meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he literally beat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, beat me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He beat you because--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was so religious. It was the biggest crime to eat non-kosher, anything that's not kosher. He was very religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was religious, but not as-- as my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a member of any Hasidic group? There were Hasidic groups in Radzyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He was a member of the [? Rydzyna ?] [? Rydzyna ?] [? rabbinate. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And what was the rabbi's name of the community? Gershon Einach. He was-- at that time, we thought in Radzyn that he's the only rabbi in the whole world, because when his birthday was, there must have come thousands and thousands from England, France, and the United-- from all over the world. And they would set up tents because there was no hotels. And even, as I remember, the president of Poland, Pilsudksi, came to-- to visit him. He was very, very famous. One coincident, I'm gonna say what happened in my house. We were sleeping. My bother came home about around maybe, I don't know, 10, 11 o'clock at night, because in there when it gets dark, you got to bed, because there was no electricity and so forth, whatever. And as my brother come home, I got up and I had to go outside to-- how do you say it? To go to the bathroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: To OK. And as I got up, I fell down. When I fell down, so my brother started to scream that I passed out. So other-- the other families started to get up-- brothers and sister. They all fell down. So my brother right away knew it whats was goin' on. So he pulled us outside, and we ran into the Polish family, which is within 50 feet, 100 feet, and they all came out and they took us in, because we were already almost dying from the gas. They were cooking with coal, and I guess they closed up. And the gas, there was no exhaust, [INAUDIBLE] exhaust. And we-- a miracle that he came home and he woke me up, that we are alive. OK. And as my brothers and sisters grew up, my brother started to be also in the same business, but he was very respectful and very liked by the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the business of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of peddlin'.

speaker: interviewer
text: Being a peddler.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did already a-- a better style of business. The people liked him. So as he started to make a living, he already started to get my mother some money for food for the other family. Also, one brother moved-- went to Warsaw. And he started to work there as a tailor. And as I understood, that he was living on bread and coffee, or tea and butter. He wouldn't go out to eat, because he want to save every penny when it came to a holiday to bring some money that we could have a holiday to buy food. And he even brought home for my father and my mother some clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as we got older, things started to improve. As the older children started working--

speaker: interviewee
text: As-- That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and helping out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Things started to improve.

speaker: interviewer
text: Things got better. What was their religious observances?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: None of-- none of your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's put that way, according to the United States, [LAUGHS] we were all religious. But according to my father we were not religious. We didn't do little things that we did, we shouldn't do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we do believed in God, but not as extreme as-- I remember I went to one of the villages where my father was dealing. And the Christian family was very happy when they gave me some non-kosher food to eat, like bacon or ham. And I'd-- I'd been so hungry I would eat anything. But they were happy that they took a Jew, they made him non-kosher. But in terms of observances, you were-- when you were growing up, were you going to shul? Were you wearing tefillin in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My father would make me, if I liked it or not, to put on the tefillin every morning. And he would take me to the-- Friday night we would all have to go to a mikvah. And we would have to get ready before sundown to go to shul. And we did it. As-- as long that he had the-- the power over us. When we grew up already, like my brother, I don't think he influenced him as much as when we were younger.

speaker: interviewer
text: I should ask you to tell us the names of your brothers and sisters since we'll be talking more about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My oldest sister was [? Sura. ?] After [? Sura ?] was born, came my brother Moishe. And after Moishe came Esther. And I think some time-- I don't remember 100% who is first and who is second. And after Esther came Itzel. And after Itzel came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came Luzer. [? Gittel ?] I think. [? Gittel ?] No. Esther. Yeah. Itzel, [? Gittel ?] then came Luzer, and then Srulke and then me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am the youngest one. We were eight children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at around what age did you start helping out, and working, and trying to help the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 18 years old, 17 years old. That's the time I started to work, which I would go get about 30, 40 cents for the day workin'. Hard work. I would go in in a basement, throw out dirt with a shovel-- I didn't know anything-- just to make something. And the work was good, because I knew after the day I'm gonna have enough money to go buy bread. And I also remember I started to work for a Polish man as a cabinet man. He was making cabinets. The first thing he did, he brought me a half a loaf of bread. And he said to me that the wife is bringin' out coffee. But before she came with the coffee, the half a loaf of bread was gone. Just with the saliva I swallowed it down. When he saw me, he felt so bad that for lunch he gave me another half a loaf of bread. And after I worked for this man a certain time, than I went to work to-- to another gentleman who was the alcoholic. And I was working, because he was makin' caskets. So-- and he was a genius of a mechanic. He could do anything, but he was an alcoholic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I worked for him a certain time. And before you know, then I went for peddling also. And then the war broke out, right? That was 1939. You mentioned-- let's, before we talk about the war, you mentioned that the anti-Semitism in the towns would stop you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from going to a certain school.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very extreme.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though only 25 or 30% was Christian, and the majority was Jewish, you still had the anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would it show itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: For an example, there would be a guy. And he would walk on the sidewalk, and-- and his elbow would totally stick out. If a Jew would pass, he would just hit him. This was goin' on constantly. You could go tell the police, it didn't mean nothing. One time I passed by, I see [? about ?] three guys beating up one guy-- beating him up only for one reason. He's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I [? mixed in ?] I went in to-- to help him out. And as I helped him out, and I walked away, you could even see it, maybe, if you look close, and I felt a knife in my-- in my face. As I turned around, one guy hit me with a knife on the back. And I was bleeding, but I survived. It wasn't that bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: But to make a story that many times the Jewish people would have to-- we used to have instead curtains or anything, we used to have, outside, we used to have wood covered the glass. Like swinging-- I don't know how to explain that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shutters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shutters. That's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And many times they were closed, because they would throw in rocks in the windows. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I gave you an example. The people who lived-- who was my friend, who-- who told me to go work with him in the field, this was a [INAUDIBLE]. And he liked me. And he said, Hitler's comin', but, Jack, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you I'll save. The other Jews, I'll help Hitler. That was his-- to show his love to me, to tell me that he's gonna help kill all other Jews, but he's gonna save me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this was during the 1930s.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1939, just before-- just before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask. During all the years leading up to the war, from 1933 to 1939--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939 was a tremendous anti-Semitism. It was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: But even before that, were you worried about what was happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We only knew-- about-- we didn't-- that's a good question. We didn't know too much before. Like, I didn't know-- maybe a year, six months before. But never did I ever dream that something like that would happen. You know, we knew it was problems, you know. But Holocaust, to-- to wipe, to take off a race from the-- from the world, just like that to destroy because you were Jewish, I never dreamt of.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one other question before we talk about what happened in 1939. What kind of groups did you belong to as a teenager?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It started out that one of my brothers was a big Communist leader in the town. He was arrested many times just before May the 1st, because that's was the celebration. May the 1st the Communists would celebrate. And he had a Polish guy working with him in the Communist party. What looks like that, this Polish guy gave it over to the police as workin' as a spy for the police. And they would arrest him. And when they would take him from prison to the police station to question, [? he ?] would pull his hair outta his head and throw it on the street. People should see it. And they also gave him needles under the nails for him to give out secrets from the Communists. But when the war broke out, and he escaped to Russia-- and I'm gonna jump now from this time to further-- and when he was in Russia, and then he immigrated to Israel. And when I met him in Israel and I asked him about Communism, he was-- nobody's anti-Communism as he turned anti-Communism, because he was an idealist. It turned out that Communism is a dictatorship, that Communism does not work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to go back, my other brother, did not-- Moishe, did not belong to any organization. And I belonged to Shomer ha-Tsa'ir. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one time that was in 1938, I guess, or '37 or '38, we were gonna go to Israel. They would pick so many. Every year they would pick so many people to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought that time was my, but somehow I didn't fall in in that group that time yet. And some of them at that time, my friends there who belonged to that group, went to Israel to be [NON-ENGLISH], because I think it's the-- to be a guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this, Shomer ha-Tsa'ir was a Zionist group. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: A Zionist group. Who--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you very much want to go to Israel and it was just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --by chance who went?

speaker: interviewee
text: They all-- they all were happy if they know they gonna be goin' there. I mean, I was very disappointed that I couldn't go to Israel. But-- but I understood the [NON-ENGLISH] means to be a guard-- you know, to guard the country. And, you know, and to build a country. And but they all, every one who went, you could see smiles on their faces. Only [? sadder ?] that we had to leave other guys behind them, or other girls, before they went. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of activities did the group do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, activities also was we would met, and I would do get some teachings from-- we would go out, like [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know if you know what like [NON-ENGLISH] means. It's a Zionist picnic, to go out to the-- in the woods and the field. And we-- we would-- we would talk in the woods. Be a lot of teaching, because the education system was very bad for Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but some of them were luckier than I because, I guess, their parents were richer, and they could send the kids more to school. And-- but the poor people had it tough to do anything. So in the-- when I belonged to Shomer ha-Tsa'ir, I would be taught some education. Let's move to 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. 1939 was-- was-- the town was very-- I don't know how to in English to explain. We knew that's comin', because they are already, we heard on the radio. There was only one radio where they were-- I remember there was a store, they was selling bikes. And there was one on the front door, was a small little radio. And you could hear that, that Hitler's preparing for-- for war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we all saw it that what's his name? The foreign diplomat from England would meet Hitler, and they would try to talk him out of it. And that was with the whole time was very, very bad. As the time the war broke out, the planes started to come. OK. Why don't we take a break now? And when we begin again, you'll tell us how that went. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is it 30 minutes?
